full_text:
Pharmacist warns heatwave could affect common medicines as temperatures reach 34C
A pharmacist is urging people taking common prescription medicines to take extra precautions during the UK heatwave, warning some drugs can increase the risk of dehydration and heat-related illness.
People taking common prescription medicines are being urged to take extra care during the UK heatwave, with a pharmacist warning that high temperatures can increase the risk of dehydration, dizziness and heat-related illness.
Pharmacist warns common medicines may affect how the body copes in hot weather
As temperatures are forecast to reach 34C in parts of the UK, pharmacist Raj Patel said some medicines can affect the body's ability to regulate temperature, maintain fluid balance or control blood sugar, making it more difficult to cope in extreme heat.
The warning applies to several commonly prescribed medicines, including furosemide, ramipril, losartan, amlodipine, bisoprolol, oxybutynin, amitriptyline, sertraline, empagliflozin and insulin.
Patel, who has more than 35 years of experience, said in a TikTok video: "If you take regular medicines, a heatwave can affect you more than you realise. This is not panic. This is not a reason to stop your tablets. This is a reason to plan ahead."
He added: "Some medicines can make heat more risky by affecting fluid balance, sweating, blood pressure, heart rate, kidneys, blood sugar or how your body cools itself."
Diuretics such as furosemide can increase the risk of dehydration by removing excess fluid from the body, while medicines used to treat high blood pressure, including ramipril and losartan, may increase the likelihood of dizziness, low blood pressure or kidney strain if a person becomes dehydrated.
Other medicines, including the beta blocker bisoprolol and bladder treatment oxybutynin, can affect how the body responds to heat by slowing the heart rate or reducing sweating, making it harder to cool down.
People with diabetes are also being advised to take extra care.
Patel said insulin and empagliflozin require particular attention during hot weather because dehydration, increased activity and heat can affect blood sugar levels.
The NHS recommends storing unopened insulin in a refrigerator and carrying insulin currently in use in a cool bag or insulated pouch when outdoors.
Patel stressed that patients should continue taking their prescribed medicines unless advised otherwise by a healthcare professional.
He said: "Do not stop prescribed medicines suddenly unless a healthcare professional tells you to."
Instead, he advised people to stay hydrated, keep cool, plan ahead and ensure medicines are stored according to their instructions.
